title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Indications
content: Several key characteristics should be considered when selecting sealers and core obturation materials. Firstly, most sealers are soluble and subject to dissolution when they contact tissue fluids. [32] Additionally, most sealers and core materials irritate the periapical tissues if extruded past the apex, except bioceramic materials, as these materials are biocompatible and rarely act as irritants. [20] [33] Next, sealers differ in their ability to adhere to prepared dentinal walls, with sealers such as ZOE being nonadherent, whereas resin-based sealers are. [9] [34] Lastly, most sealers experience shrinkage upon setting and can create void space between the core material and the dentinal wall, except bioceramic sealers exhibit expansion upon setting. [9] [22] Due to bioceramic materials' enhanced biocompatible and physical properties, they have been indicated and utilized in most cases in modern endodontics. [5] [21] However, using a specific sealer or core material depends entirely on the operator's preference and can be case-specific.

title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Technique -- Core Obturation Materials
content: The master cone is inserted into the canal to the working length, followed by the spreader, which, if properly selected, should reach 2 mm shorter. The spreader is then rotated and withdrawn from the canal, and the matching gutta-percha cone is inserted into the vacant space. [39] The process is repeated until the operator cannot insert the spreader deeper than the coronal third of the prepared canal. The excess gutta-percha is seared off with a heated instrument and compacted using a root canal plugger. [5] [41] [39]

title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Technique -- Core Obturation Materials
content: Since there are numerous types of obturation materials, there are several ways to insert them into the root canal, and as a result, this section focuses primarily on the insertion technique of gutta-percha. Gutta-percha has 2 main obturation techniques, cold lateral condensation and warm vertical compaction. [5] [39] The cold lateral condensation technique involves the selection of a master gutta-percha cone, the use of spreaders, and the insertion of secondary gutta-percha cones. [5] [40] The master cone is a gutta-percha cone with a taper and diameter that matches the prepared root canal at the working length. This master cone is measured to the working length and inserted into the canal, where it displays slight resistance to removal, aptly referred to as "tug back" if the correct size cone is selected. [5] After confirming with a radiograph, the canal is irrigated and dried, and sealer is applied to the walls. At this point, a finger spreader or a hand spreader is pre-fit in the canal to reach within 1 to 2 mm short of the working length, and appropriately sized secondary gutta-percha cones are selected that closely match the size of the spreader. [39]

title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Introduction
content: Root canals are 3D structures with complex and unique anatomy; no 2 are alike. Achieving a 3D obturation with complete coronal, lateral, and apical seals is essential for long-term success. [1] Radiographs are still the main method used to visualize root canals during the diagnostic and treatment stages. However, one of their major weaknesses is that they only provide a 2D representation of 3D canals. [3] As a result, it is impossible to assess the quality of the canal seal created during the obturation stage. Introducing micro-CT imaging into the field of endodontics has dramatically enhanced the accuracy of diagnosis, cleaning shaping, and, ultimately, obturation. [4] There are countless materials and techniques for obturation. Most techniques employ a sealer and a core filling material to ensure complete obturation. This activity discusses available sealers and proceeds to core obturation materials.

title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Classification -- Root Canal Sealers
content: The role of the sealer is critical for establishing a fluid-tight seal of the root canal as it fills the vacant space between the prepared dentinal wall and the core filling material. [1] [5] It also reaches lateral canals and tubules inaccessible to gutta-percha cones and provides antimicrobial action. [5] Most sealers are toxic before setting, so care should be taken to avoid the extrusion of sealers into the periapical tissues. [6] Sealers induce different levels of periapical inflammation; however, this effect is usually transient and does not interfere with tissue healing. [7] Sealers are partly reabsorbed after exposure to tissue fluids. The core material should occupy almost all of the canal, and sealers should only be applied in small quantities. Several types of sealers are available, which vary in formulation and properties. They are classified into 5 main groups according to their composition: zinc oxide eugenol (ZOE) based, calcium hydroxide based, glass ionomer-based, resin-based, and bioceramic sealers. The properties of an ideal sealer have been outlined in the literature. It is important to note that no material has been proven to eliminate leakage nor fulfill all the ideals.

title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Properties -- ZOE Sealers
content: ZOE-based sealers are the most used sealers in clinical endodontics. They present as a powder-liquid or a 2-paste preparation. [5] Although ex vivo laboratory studies have demonstrated cytotoxic properties in ZOE sealers due to the presence of eugenol, they have proven to be clinically useful in both animal and human studies. [5] [9] Notable properties of ZOE sealers include absorption if extruded into the surrounding tissues and anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial effects. [10] However, ZOE sealers become weak and porous upon setting, making them susceptible to decomposition if tissue fluids leak. [9] [10] Alternatively, other sealers that do not include eugenol are available and, therefore, mitigate any of its potentially deleterious effects. These sealers are aptly referred to as non-eugenol sealers.

title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Properties -- Bioceramic Sealers
content: Bioceramic sealers are biocompatible materials that use either calcium silicate or calcium phosphate as their main constituent. [21] Mineral trioxide aggregate (MTA) is a new material currently used as a root canal sealer, in addition to its many other uses within the field of endodontics. The formula of MTA is primarily tricalcium silicate with added bismuth oxide, a radiopaque powder for enhanced visualization on radiography. [21] [22] MTA is activated with the introduction of water and produces a highly alkaline mixture with a pH of 12 and beneficial calcium silicate hydrates. These hydrates make MTA an excellent endodontic material as they form on the surface of existing calcium silicate particles and continue to grow inwards, effectively creating a barrier. [21] [22] One major disadvantage of bioceramic sealers is that the enhanced adhesion between the material and the dentin wall makes endodontic retreatment and post-preparation increasingly challenging. [21] [22]

title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Technique -- Root Canal Sealers
content: Several techniques are used to insert the sealer into the root canal and ensure it evenly coats the prepared surface. One technique utilizes a screw-like file that attaches to a slow-speed handpiece called lentulo spiral. [36] The lentulo spiral is dipped into the sealer and inserted into the root canal. When the handpiece is activated, the lentulo spiral spins and evenly distributes the sealer on the walls through centrifugal force. [37] Its use has several downsides, including the risk of instrument fracture due to the instrument being very fragile and easily breakable. [38] [36] Additionally, it has been shown to accelerate the setting of resin-based sealers due to the stirring effect. [19] Other popular techniques for inserting sealer include the use of gutta-percha cones. The operator butters the pre-fit paper point or gutta-percha cone in the sealer and then inserts it in the canal using a pumping action to ensure even coating. [1]

title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Contraindications
content: There are few true contraindications for sealers and core obturation materials. One major contraindication is an allergy to any component of the material. [35] Allergies to constituent materials are rare but possible in every sealer and core material discussed, and the operator should take care to review all of a patient's possible allergies and be able to respond should a medical emergency arise. Other non-absolute contraindications are case-specific and depend on the treatment goal of the operator.

title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Properties -- Other Core Materials
content: There are various other core obturation materials commercially available; however, no statistical difference in terms of reduction in leakage has been noted between them and gutta-percha. [1] [5] These materials include GI-impregnated gutta-percha cones and resin-based obturation systems. [30] Using a resin core in addition to a resin sealer is believed to allow the formation of a 'monoblock,' or bonded homogenous mass of material within the root canal. [31] However, this has not yet been achieved with current materials. [31]

title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Properties -- Glass Ionomer Sealers
content: Clinicians advocating using glass ionomer sealers as an obturation material do so due to their dentin-bonding properties. [15] Dentin bonding results from the reaction between the polyacrylic acid in the glass ionomer and the hydroxyapatite on the dentinal wall and allows for excellent adhesion between the obturation material and the prepared canal wall. [16] Several major advantages arise from the improved adherence, including superior apical sealing, increased resistance to root fracture, and resistance to solubility in tissue fluids. [16] [17] Additional advantages include antimicrobial properties and sustained fluoride release. However, the dentinal walls are hard to reach with primer solutions in the middle and apical thirds, potentially limiting the adhesion of the sealer to the walls. [17] [18] Furthermore, the dentin-bonded glass ionomer sealer creates a homogenous solid material that is difficult to remove during endodontic retreatment. [18]

title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Introduction
content: Filling root canals, commonly called obturation, is a critical step in successful treatment. The goals of obturation are twofold: to create an apical seal and fill the root canal without incorporating voids within the filling material. [1] [2] Successful obturation eliminates leakage of contaminants into the channel, such as saliva, bacteria, and periapical tissue fluid, and entraps any residual microorganisms within the canal space. [2] The ability to adequately obturate a root canal is directly related to the preceding step of cleaning and shaping. Cleaning and shaping refer to a root canal's mechanical and chemical preparation to remove organic and inorganic matter. A canal that has been poorly cleaned and shaped results in equally poor obturation and is one of the strongest indicators of treatment failure. [2]

title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Properties -- Gutta-Percha
content: Gutta-percha has many advantages, such as ease of manipulation, radiopacity, low toxicity, and plasticity. However, it does not bond to dentin and shrinks when cooled. [5] [25] Gutta-percha cones are available in standardized 0.02 taper as specified by the International Organization of Standardization and non-standardized sizes. [5] This allows clinicians to mechanically prepare the canal space using various commercial rotary systems using standardized file sizes. When the canal is prepared to a given file size, a gutta-percha cone of the same size can be inserted and adapted to the canal. [1] [5] [25]

title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Properties -- Silver Cones
content: Silver cones (also referred to as silver points) are a historic obturation material that is no longer used in modern endodontics. The original basis for their use stemmed from the belief that they had an oligodynamic property (heavy metals' capacity to exert a bactericidal effect) that led to the destruction of intracanal microbes. [5] This belief has been proven to be false, and silver points have been shown to have several major flaws as obturation materials. [23] One major disadvantage is that they corrode when exposed to saliva or tissue fluid, creating cytotoxic products, and do not fill most root canals, resulting in leakage. [23] [24]

title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Classification -- Core Obturation Materials
content: Core materials exist to fill the bulk of the root canal space and act as a plug to prevent any leakage or ingress of foreign material. They take up most of the area within the canal and rely on the sealer to fill any voids or irregular spaces between it and the canal walls. Although other materials have been fabricated recently, gutta-percha remains the most widely used and accepted core filling material. Properties of an ideal core obturation material: Ease of manipulation Dimensional stability Excellent sealing ability (apically and laterally) Non-irritating to periapical tissues Radiopaque (to be visible on radiographs) Non-staining or discoloring Antimicrobial Biocompatible [1] [5]

title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Properties -- Resin Sealers
content: Resin sealers are non-eugenol sealers that provide excellent adhesion to the canal walls. [9] These sealers can be separated based on their constituent elements, such as epoxy resin-based or methacrylate resin-based sealers. Methacrylate resin-based sealers are further classified into 4 distinct generations, each with its unique formulation. The generations vary according to primer type and non-etching vs. self-etching capabilities. [9] [19] Resin-based sealers have several beneficial properties, including excellent adhesion and seal and good tolerability from apical tissues. [9] [19] However, it has also been shown to produce an initial inflammatory reaction with the potential for allergic and mutagenic responses. [20]

title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Clinical Significance
content: Root canal obturation aims to ensure that any residual microorganisms remaining after canal cleaning and disinfection are completely entombed, thus preventing future proliferation and subsequent reinfection. [5] A well-made obturation prevents bacteria or possible nutrients from the oral cavity and periodontal and periapical fluids from reaching the root canal system. [5]

title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Properties -- Bioceramic Materials
content: Bioceramics, particularly MTA, can also be a core filling material. In addition to the aforementioned chemical properties of MTA, it is commonly used as an endodontic material due to its many beneficial characteristics. These include a long set time, a low relative compressive strength, high fracture resistance, and antibacterial and antifungal properties. [26] [27] Furthermore, MTA materials have been consistently proven in the literature to be biocompatible due to their high pH and ability to form hydroxyapatite, an organic substance that promotes bony growth. [28] There are several notable uses of MTA in endodontics, including in the repair of perforations during endodontic treatment, internal root resorption, apexogenesis, and pulp capping procedures. [11] [29]

title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Technique -- Core Obturation Materials
content: The warm vertical compaction technique uses a master gutta-percha cone, Schilder pluggers, and a heat source. [42] [40] [42] The master cone in this method should fit just short of the working length and have a slight tug back. After confirming with a radiograph, the canal is irrigated and dried, and sealer is applied to the walls. The master cone is reinserted, and the coronal portion is removed with a heated instrument. [42] Next, a heated plugger is used to gradually and sequentially remove portions of the coronal gutta-percha while softening the cone below. [42] The heated plugger is removed from the canal, a non-heated plugger is inserted, and the warm gutta-percha is compacted to force the now-plasticized material apically. [42] This process is repeated until 4 to 5 mm of adapted gutta-percha remains at the apex, forming an effective seal. [40] Small gutta-percha cones are inserted into the canal, heated with a heated plugger, and then compacted with a non-heated plugger. This is repeated until the canal is fully obturated with plasticized gutta-percha. [5] [42] [5] [39]

The prepared cavity reflects the shape of the canal system, supporting round, tapered filling materials placed into the apical third. Circumferential filling instruments or Gates-Glidden drills are then employed to clean and shape the remaining ovoid portion of the canal, ensuring adequate debridement and proper preparation for obturation. title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Roots -- Technique or Treatment
content: Endodontic File Technique: Using endodontic files to retrieve retained root segments involves the insertion of an endodontic file through the visible root canal orifice from the coronal aspect and advancing it apically until friction develops within the canal. [23] The more contact surface area the file has within the canal, the more generated force when pulling the root segment coronally. [23] The type and size of endodontic file best suited for extraction in root tips have yet to be well-studied, but it has been shown that a #25 Hedstrom file generates a tremendous pullout force in all roots, regardless of the length of the root. Factors such as root length and PDL attachment significantly impact the use of this technique. Shorter root segments and tips offer the greatest likelihood of technique success. Barbed broaches, size #40 or #50 endodontic reamers, and even file sizes up to #80 were all identified as potential adjuncts. [23] [24] However, larger file sizes may restrict the distance of apical advancement of the file within the canal, limiting the amount of frictional force. Also, due to the significant amount of friction generated within the canal, imprudent amounts of rotational force can cause files to break, further complicating the extraction. [25]

title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2039
content: Iron in other combinations is classified as an iron antianemic preparation. Iron antianemic preparations are therapeutic agents used to replenish iron stores and treat iron‑deficiency anemia. Formulations described as “iron in other combinations” contain iron together with additional compounds or nutrients, enhancing absorption or providing complementary benefits while still serving the primary purpose of correcting iron deficiency. Recognizing these combination products as a subset of iron antianemic preparations helps clinicians select appropriate therapy for patients with anemia, especially when co‑existing deficiencies or specific tolerability issues are present.

title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2120
content: Vitamin K is classified as a fat‑soluble vitamin, meaning it belongs to the broader group of vitamins that dissolve in lipids and require dietary fat for optimal absorption. This places vitamin K within the same category as vitamins A, D, and E, highlighting shared characteristics such as storage in fatty tissues and the need for adequate fat intake to ensure proper bioavailability. Recognizing vitamin K as a fat‑soluble vitamin is essential for understanding its absorption dynamics, its role in physiological processes like blood coagulation, and for guiding nutritional recommendations that support its effectiveness.
